coredata.dc:description: Literature evidence reveals versatile applications of carbon dots (CDs), but generally mixtures of various types of carbon nanomaterials, molecular intermediates as well as side products are obtained upon hydrothermal treatment of the precursor material. This demands isolation of pure components and their complete characterization before these nano carbonaceous materials are chosen for suitable applications. In the present study, perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA) is subjected to hydrothermal treatment and the mother liquor obtained is separated using column chromatography technique using dichloromethane-methanol solvent system to isolate fractions of various fluorescent carbonaceous nanostructures. The TEM imaging of nano carbonaceous particles of all five fractions indicated that the first and third fractions were composed of nanoribbons, while the latter two fractions largely contained quasi-spherical nanoparticles of both lesser (carbon quantum dots) and greater (carbon nanodots) than 10 nm dimensions. The XPS results of all the fractions suggested separation based on polarity difference. The ID/IG ratios obtained from Raman spectra implied the presence of several defects on the CDs. The time resolved fluorescence spectra of third, fourth and fifth fractions revealed mono-exponential decay of fluorophores with excitation independent average lifetime values. The fifth fraction exhibited good biocompatibility and the highest absolute fluorescence quantum yield of 58.47 % among all the isolated samples. As these CDs displayed a remarkable rise in the quantum yield to 88.60 % when dispersed in water, a water-based flexo-ink was formulated. The photostable pale yellow flexo print proofs obtained on UV dull paper exhibited a green fluorescence under 365 nm illumination, whereas a yellow glow when shined with blue light, which can serve as an authentication feature for security documents and currency notes. Moreover, as the third fraction constitutes mainly of carbon nanoribbons (CNRs), an optimized polymer electrolyte was prepared along with sodium alginate (SA), and MgCl2 to understand their potential use in energy storage application. A supercapacitor was fabricated and tested for its electrochemical performance such as cyclic voltammtery (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD). An enhanced current window was observed in the CV of SA/CNRs compared to pure SA and SA/CNRs/Mg films, which indicated a structural interaction of CNRs with SA. The conductivity of SA/CNRs/Mg was lesser than SA/CNRs in EIS studies due to the presence of Mg ions, while pure SA showed lesser conductivity. The dual ionic interaction of Na and Mg along with enhanced structural stability due to doped CNRs favors its convenient supercapacitor application. The fabricated eco-friendly supercapacitor showed a specific capacitance of 84 F/g. The GCD of the device displayed pseudocapacitance behaviour and was quite stable for 2000 cycles with coulombic efficiency of 96 %.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Plausible synthetic scheme for carbonaceous nanomaterials from PTCDA dye [18].
UV spectra of (a) NMP, PTCDA in NMP & ML and (b) column purified fractions 1–5.
2D PL spectra of (a) PTCDA in NMP, (b) ML, (c) DCM (solvent) and (d) first, (e) second, (f) third, (g) fourth and (h) fifth fractions.

(a) TEM image and (b) SAED pattern for CDs in the first fraction. (c) TEM image of CDs in second fraction and (d) their SAED pattern. (e) TEM image of CNRs along with few CDs and (f) SAED pattern for CNRs in fraction three. (g) TEM image of CDs in fourth fraction along with lattice infringes and (h) SAED pattern for polycrystalline CDs. (i) TEM image of CDs in fifth fraction and corresponding lattice infringes and (j) SAED pattern.

(a) Survey spectra of ML, second, third, fourth and fifth fractions. Full scan spectra of (b) C1s & (c) O1s of ML, (d) C1s & (e) O1s of second fraction, (f) C1s & (g) O1s of third fraction, (h) C1s, (i) O1s & (j) N1s of fourth fraction and (k) C1s & (l) O1s of fifth fraction.

(a) XRD and (b) Raman spectra of ML, second, third, fourth and fifth fractions. Time resolved fluorescence spectra of fifth fraction recorded at (c) λex = 420 nm, (d) λex = 450 nm and (e) λex = 480 nm.

Fluorescence spectra of (a) flexo ink formulation (inset image: ink under daylight & 365 nm UV light), (b) control ink formulation (inset image: control ink under daylight & 365 nm UV light), (c) coated and (d) printed paper samples. Images of ‘MIT’ written on UV dull paper using the flexo ink and viewed under (e) daylight, (f) 365 nm UV light and (g) blue light.

CVs at different scan rates of (a) pure SA, (b) SA/CNRs, (c) SA/CNRs/Mg and (d) comparison. (e) Nyquist plots of SA, SA/CNRs and SA/CNRs/Mg. (f) CVs of supercapacitor at different scan rates and (g) Nyquist Plot of supercapacitor. (h) GCD curves at different current density (a) 0.2 mA, (b) 0.4 mA, (c) 0.6 mA, and (d) 0.8 mA.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Literature evidence reveals versatile applications of carbon dots (CDs), but generally mixtures of various types of carbon nanomaterials, molecular intermediates as well as side products are obtained upon hydrothermal treatment of the precursor material. This demands isolation of pure components and their complete characterization before these nano carbonaceous materials are chosen for suitable applications. In the present study, perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA) is subjected to hydrothermal treatment and the mother liquor obtained is separated using column chromatography technique using dichloromethane-methanol solvent system to isolate fractions of various fluorescent carbonaceous nanostructures. The TEM imaging of nano carbonaceous particles of all five fractions indicated that the first and third fractions were composed of nanoribbons, while the latter two fractions largely contained quasi-spherical nanoparticles of both lesser (carbon quantum dots) and greater (carbon nanodots) than 10 nm dimensions. The XPS results of all the fractions suggested separation based on polarity difference. The ID/IG ratios obtained from Raman spectra implied the presence of several defects on the CDs. The time resolved fluorescence spectra of third, fourth and fifth fractions revealed mono-exponential decay of fluorophores with excitation independent average lifetime values. The fifth fraction exhibited good biocompatibility and the highest absolute fluorescence quantum yield of 58.47 % among all the isolated samples. As these CDs displayed a remarkable rise in the quantum yield to 88.60 % when dispersed in water, a water-based flexo-ink was formulated. The photostable pale yellow flexo print proofs obtained on UV dull paper exhibited a green fluorescence under 365 nm illumination, whereas a yellow glow when shined with blue light, which can serve as an authentication feature for security documents and currency notes. Moreover, as the third fraction constitutes mainly of carbon nanoribbons (CNRs), an optimized polymer electrolyte was prepared along with sodium alginate (SA), and MgCl2 to understand their potential use in energy storage application. A supercapacitor was fabricated and tested for its electrochemical performance such as cyclic voltammtery (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD). An enhanced current window was observed in the CV of SA/CNRs compared to pure SA and SA/CNRs/Mg films, which indicated a structural interaction of CNRs with SA. The conductivity of SA/CNRs/Mg was lesser than SA/CNRs in EIS studies due to the presence of Mg ions, while pure SA showed lesser conductivity. The dual ionic interaction of Na and Mg along with enhanced structural stability due to doped CNRs favors its convenient supercapacitor application. The fabricated eco-friendly supercapacitor showed a specific capacitance of 84 F/g. The GCD of the device displayed pseudocapacitance behaviour and was quite stable for 2000 cycles with coulombic efficiency of 96 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Supplementary Data provided. The datasets generated during and/or analysed during the current study are available from the first author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: After their discovery as a byproduct during the synthesis of carbon nano tubes [1], carbon dots (CDs) including carbon quantum dots, graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and carbonized polymer dots [2–4] have been extensively prepared from wide variety of precursors using different synthesis approaches. Exploration of the literature unravel ample and extensive studies on carbon core structure, surface functional groups, chemical, physical, and photophysical properties of carbon nanomaterials for diverse applications including biosensing, bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, solar cells, anti-counterfeiting etc. [5–12]. However, most of these studies report the use of as-synthesized CD samples having complex composition; mixtures of several CD fractions along with other molecular intermediates and side products with significantly different properties, which might affect the real-life applications of these unique nanomaterials. Therefore, knowledge about the true composition of these emergent nano carbonaceous materials is of crucial importance as inadequate purification poses a primary setback in their versatile applicability.

The heterogeneous composition hampers the explicit analysis of the CD sample for practical application as it can only represent the average features of the individual CD species. Moreover, the isolation of pure homogeneous CDs could significantly increase the operational efficiency of CD-based devices. The morphology, chemical composition, and fundamental properties of CDs can be better understood by adopting various analytical techniques to isolate pure CDs of known composition to establish their error-free practical applications in different fields [10]. Thus, consistent purification steps need to be implemented to enable a strategic shift that would unlock the tangible capability of these carbonaceous nanoparticles as next-generation smart materials spanning various applications. Though many studies report centrifugation and filtration for the purification of CDs, these methods have been frequently demonstrated as inadequate separation techniques [13,14]. Besides, despite the convenience dialysis of as-prepared CDs using too low molecular weight cut off inappropriate membranes or adopting less duration for the process could result in incomplete purification [6,7]. Thus, there is a persistent demand to implement reliable and adequate purification approaches to reduce sample complexity and provide reliable results towards the advancement of CDs as next-generation functional materials. Few research groups have reported the most analytically sound chromatographic techniques to reduce the complexity of the CD mixture through fractionation process. However, presently these reports constitute only less than 10 % of the reported CD purification processes and there is no detailed study on column chromatographic isolation of carbonaceous nanomaterials and the detailed evaluation of structural and morphological analysis of the fractions obtained.

Aiming at a complete shift towards fabrication of engineered carbonaceous nanomaterials for targeted real-life utility we report the hydrothermal synthesis of CDs from hydrophobic perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA) dye and further purification of the mother liquor through column chromatography approach to obtain five fractions of varying CD composition. Further, the morphological, structural, and chemical nature of the isolated fractions are carefully analyzed using various characterization techniques. Furthermore, a water-based ink formulation is prepared from fluorescent CDs of fifth fraction for flexography printing. The photostability of the printed sample and its suitability for security printing are investigated. Moreover, the CDs of fraction three are investigated in energy storage application using a polymer electrolyte film constituting sodium alginate (SA), and MgCl2. The supercapacitor is tested using cyclic voltammtery (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and galvanostatic charge/discharge studies (GCD).

About 1 M NaOH (molecular weight: 40.0 g/mol) solution (2 g dissolved in 5 mL de-ionized water) was mixed with PTCDA (392.32 g/mol, Sigma Aldrich; 50 mg) in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (99.14 g/mol, Molychem; NMP: 5 mL) and the total volume was made up to 10 mL with de-ionized water in a 25 mL Teflon coated autoclave. The hydrothermal reaction was set-up for 1 h at 155 °C and the resultant mother liquor (ML) obtained was filtered to remove the suspended solids. A column of length 150 mm with capacity of 35 mL purchased from ABG INITIATIVE was prepared via wet packing method for the separation of CDs. The slurry was prepared using 20 g of 400–700 meshwork silica gel. Dichloromethane (DCM)-methanol solvent system was chosen based on the separation of ML in thin layer chromatography (TLC) plates. The synthesis and purification steps were repeated for confirming reproducible results (n = 10).

The absorption and emission spectra were recorded using Shimadzu UV–visible spectrophotometer and JASCO spectrofluorometer FP-8300 instrument, Japan (excitation and emission bandwidths set between 2.5 and 1 nm). The fluorescence lifetime values were measured in nanoseconds using a DeltaFlex TCSPC Lifetime Fluorimeter, USA equipped with conventional PMT detector set with a 13 ps per bin (channel), data resolution and instrument response function of ∼70 ps. TEM/HRTEM images and SAED patterns were obtained using JEM 2100F model, Japan with an electron source of 200 kV. The characteristic functional groups were identified using ATR-IR Shimadzu-IR Spirit analyzer, Japan with 40 scans at 4 cm−1 resolution. The XRD spectra were recorded using Rigaku Miniflex 600 (5th gen), Japan. The elements were detected using Omicron XPS system (Nanotechnology Ltd., Germany) using non-monochromatic Mg-Kα and Al-Kα as the excitation sources. Raman spectra were recorded on I-Raman plus commercial setup from BWTEK, USA with the excitation wavelength of 785 nm for an exposure time of 30 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of flexography ink and study of print properties
ce:section.ce:para: Methanol from the fraction five was evaporated to obtain the CDs, which were re-dispersed in de-ionised water. Further, the binder which includes surfactant, defoamer and wax (weight percentage: 80) was added to the CD dispersed water (weight percentage: 20) to formulate the ink for flexography printing. The ink rheology was assessed using Rheometer, Anton-Paar, (Austria). The viscosity was studied at shear rate for 0.1–1000 s−1 at room and elevated temperatures. The grammage (in gsm) of UV dull paper used for obtaining flexo print proofs was measured using Digital GSM tester. The coating of ink on the paper substrate was obtained with a zero number K bar coater. The flexo printing was performed using a RK Flexiproof 100 instrument at 50 m/min, 9.5 BCM Anilox roll, chambered doctor blade inking equipment, and photopolymer plate.

HeLa cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM, CELL clone, India) containing 10 % Fetal Bovine Serum (Invitrogen, USA) and antibiotics (penicillin:100 units/mL and streptomycin: 100 μg/mL). Cells were incubated in a humidified 5 % CO2 incubator (Sanyo, UK) at 37 °C. Cell toxicity induced by CDs was evaluated by 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. HeLa cells were seeded (∼1 × 104 cells/ well in 96 well plate), treated with different concentrations (1, 0.8, 0.6, 0.4, 0.2, 0.1 and 0.05 mg/mL) of CDs and incubated for 24 h. Then the cells were washed, 20 μL of MTT (5 mg/mL in DMEM) was added in each well and incubated for 3 h in the dark. Finally, the medium was removed, DMSO (100 μL) was added, incubated for 5 min in the dark, and absorbance was measured at 560 nm to calculate the percentage cell viability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of polymer electrolyte and fabrication of supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: A stock solution of SA (5 g in 50 mL, Merck) was prepared. MgCl2 (Merck) was used as dopant along with the carbon nanoribbons (CNRs). Three different films: pure SA, SA/CNRs and SA/CNRs/Mg were prepared by solution casting technique. The concentration of CNRs and Mg salt was optimized for maximum conductivity without loss of film forming property. Two stainless steel (SS) plates of 1 cm × 1 cm dimension was used as current collector on which activated carbon was coated using poly(vinylidene fluoride) in NMP. The prepared SA/CNR/Mg was placed between these SS electrodes and wrapped with plastic coated aluminium (Al) foil. The electrochemical characterisation was done using Biologic SP50e instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and isolation of CD fractions
ce:section.ce:para: PTCDA is a n-type organic semiconducting hydrophobic dye which forms highly crystalline films and is used in electronic and opto-electronic applications [15,16]. The molecular structure of the dye consists of a perylene core to which two anhydride groups are attached, one at either side and provides high electron mobility due to its low intermolecular distance leading to π-π conjugation [17,18]. PTCDA can also be used to prepare aromatic fluorophores dyes and pigments for a highly reversible fluorescence switching on different substrates [19]. The red-colored oxygen rich π-conjugated hydrophobic PTCDA framework that serves as a prototype molecule for its suitability in several optoelectronic devices is chosen as the precursor molecule for the present study. During the synthesis process, the addition of aqueous NaOH to PTCDA generated the tetrasodium salt [20], and subsequent hydrothermal treatment for 1 h at 155 °C post NMP addition resulted in the formation of ML as a complex mixture of different types of carbonaceous nanoparticles. The proposed mechanism for the hydrothermal conversion of PTCDA to obtain the ML is presented in Fig. 1 . The separation of different types of pure CDs from the ML was accomplished using column chromatography. Thin layer chromatography (TLC) was performed to select the appropriate solvent elution system, and the respective photographs of the TLC plates are presented in Supplementary Figure (SF) 1. Initially, nonpolar DCM was used and subsequently the polarity was increased using methanol. Ultimately, 9:1 (DCM:methanol) and 3:7 (DCM:methanol) elution systems were chosen for column chromatography based on the excellent separation observed in TLC. Few aliquots of ML sample were fed to the top of the column and non-polar DCM was added initially. Further, the polarity was increased to 9:1 (DCM:methanol) solvent system, followed by 3:7, and eventually 100 % methanol to ensure complete elution of CD fractions (SF 2 a-d). Five different CD fractions were obtained from the PTCDA-derived ML; first and second fractions were obtained upon allowing the passage of pure DCM through the column, third fraction was collected by passing the 9:1 solvent system (DCM:methanol) followed by the fourth fraction using 7:3 (DCM:methanol) solvent system and the final fifth fraction eluted out upon adding pure methanol. The SF 2 e and f depict the photographs of the column purified fractions 1–5 under daylight and 365 nm UV illumination.

The UV spectra of PTCDA in NMP and ML are depicted in Fig. 2
a. NMP displays only one prominent absorption peak at 300 nm. In addition to the solvent peak, PTCDA exhibits absorbance peaks at 490 and 527 nm. However, three UV absorbance peaks seen at 229, 260 and 289 nm and the three peaks observed in the visible region at 415, 438 and 467 nm in the ML could be assigned to various electronic transitions (π-π* and n-π*). The differences in the absorption wavelengths of the ML sample from the precursor materials indicate chemical modifications. The characteristic absorptions of the column purified fractions 1–5 are evident from Fig. 2 b. The first fraction shows two prominent absorbance peaks corresponding to higher and lower transitions at 228 and 275 nm. Besides, less intense absorptions are perceived in the visible region (shown in the inset) at 485 and 521 nm. The absorptions of the second and third fractions are observed at 216–264 nm in addition to 495–498 nm, respectively. The fourth fraction has absorbance peaks at 296 and 332 nm. The fifth fraction displays lesser number of absorptions at 290, 437, 464 and 510 nm.

The 3D photoluminescence (PL) spectrum (SF 3 a) of PTCDA in NMP displays two excitations at 380 (marked as A) and 430 nm (marked as B) having a common emission at 580 nm. The 2D PL spectrum also depicts similar emission regions along with a shoulder band at 632 nm for excitations as shown in the Fig. 3
a. Moreover, excitation at 550 nm also induces emission response at 580 nm. The PL response for solvent NMP (SF 3 b) shows emission at 430 nm for excitation at 350 nm (marked as A) along with minor emission at 530 nm for excitation at 370 nm excitation (marked as B). However, the ML product obtained after hydrothermal reaction displays emissions at different wavelengths which indicate chemical modifications or formation of new fluorophores. The 3D PL spectrum of ML sample (SF 3 c) shows emissions at 490, 520 and 550 nm for excitation at 390 nm (marked as A). Similar emissions are observed for excitations at 450 and 480 nm as evident from 2D PL spectrum (Fig. 3 b). Further, the fluorescence emission spectra of all the column purified fractions were recorded. The first fraction eluted with DCM and hence the 3D and 2D PL spectra of DCM (SF 4 a and Fig. 3 c) and fraction 1 (SF 24 and Fig. 3 d) are compared. The solvent and first fraction display emissions at 410–415 and 430–440 nm emissions for 370 nm excitation, which majorly constitutes the solvent. Therefore, a new emission peak at 530 nm exclusively belongs to the CDs of first fraction. The 3D PL spectra of first fraction (SF 4 b) displays emission at 430 and 460 nm for excitation at 370 nm (marked as A), whereas a less intense emission centred at 530 nm corresponding to excitation at 480 nm (marked as B) is also observed. The second fraction elutes immediately after the separation of first fraction and exhibits similar emission (SF 4 c) as observed in the solvent (marked as A). The actual emission from second fraction is centred at 550 and 580 nm for excitations at 460 nm (marked as B), 490 nm (marked as C) and 520 nm (marked as D). The 2D spectrum of second fraction (Fig. 3 e) displays emissions centred at 430 and 460 nm corresponding to 370 and 390 nm excitations belonging to solvent. In addition, emissions are observed at 550 and 580 nm for excitations at 370, 390, 460, 490 and 520 nm. As PTCDA also displays emission at 580 nm, the fluorescence spectrum of second fraction indicates the presence of both unmodified fluorophores (λem = 550 nm) and modified fluorophores (λem = 580 nm). The third fraction exhibits major emission at 570 nm for excitations corresponding to 360 (marked as A), 420 (marked as B) and 540 nm (marked as C) as depicted in the 3D PL spectrum (SF 4 d). The 2D PL spectrum further depicts that multiple excitations produce a single emission peak at 570 nm (Fig. 3 f). The fluorescence spectra of the third fraction thus show no similarities with that of ML, first and second fractions. The 3D PL spectrum of fourth fraction displays presence of two emissive species as depicted in SF 4 e. The excitations at 390, 450 and 490 nm produce emissions at 486 and 560 nm indicating the presence of new fluorophores (Fig. 3 g). In addition, the fluorophores with 570 nm emission upon excitation at 530 and 540 nm are present as in the third fraction. The last fraction elutes upon addition of more polar solvent and rather exhibits presence of three emissive species corresponding to 490, 510 and 550 nm as displayed in SF 4 f. The PL spectrum however presents multiple excitations at 390, 400, 420, 450 and 480 nm along with similar emissive behaviour to ML sample (Fig. 3 h). In addition, less intense emissions at 490, 510 and 550 nm are observed for excitations at 320 and 350 nm.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) for ML and the separated fractions were recorded using absolute method using integrated sphere as depicted graphically in the bar diagram SF 5. The QY of PTCDA in NMP is measured to be the lowest with a value of 3.76 %. Subsequent hydrothermal reaction induces formation of water-soluble ML with higher QY of 31.39 %. However, the separation of ML using column chromatography resulted in polarity-based fractions with different QY. The first fraction that comprises majorly of DCM solvent exhibits the least QY of 13.25 %. The second fraction displays the highest QY of 61.68 %, probably due to the presence of highly emissive fluorophores. However, the third and fourth fractions show almost similar QY of 32.39 % and 39.30 % respectively as they both possess emitters corresponding to 570 and 560 nm. On the contrary, the fifth fraction shows QY as high as 58.47 %.

To comprehend the influence of column separation on the morphological features of CDs, TEM images were examined. The CDs present in the first fraction appear quasi-spherical (Fig. 4
a) along with few CNRs (SF 6 a). The size histogram as presented in SF 7 a indicates that CDs have average diameter of 4–8 nm. The SAED patterns (Fig. 4 b) appear as definite ring diffractions pointing towards polycrystallinity of these CDs. In addition, the diffraction patterns were measured to obtain the d-spaces values as 0.21, 0.14 and 0.12 nm respectively. The second fraction was imaged in different areas as shown in Fig. 4 c. The corresponding SAED pattern reveals the polycrystallinity nature of the CDs with measured d-space values of 0.13, 0.14, 0.21, 0.34 and 0.28 nm (Fig. 4 d). The HRTEM (SF 6 b) however, presents overlapped CDs having different lattice infringes with one of the d-space measured to be 0.32 nm corresponding to (002) plane of diffraction. The diameters of the CDs were between 1–12 nm as shown in SF 7 b. Though the third fraction contains CDs with diameter between 1 and 8 nm (SF 6 c and SF 7 c) numerous tubular CNRs as shown in Fig. 4 e are observed. The SAED pattern (Fig. 4 f) of these structures indicates polycrystalline nature of the constituents. The d-space values of the CDs were estimated as 0.12, 0.21 and 0.30 nm. However, the fourth fraction comprises mostly of quasi-spherical CDs (Fig. 4 g) having average diameter of 1–9 nm (SF 6 d). The SAED pattern (Fig. 4 h) obtained for different regions indicates polycrystallinity with d-space values of 0.12, 0.21, 0.27 and 0.30 nm. The HRTEM image of the CDs provides the lattice infringe with d-space measured as 0.32 nm belonging to (002) plane of diffraction as shown in SF 7 d. The fifth fraction comprises of CDs (Fig. 4 i) of varying diameters ranging between 10–50 nm as shown in SF 6 e. The corresponding SAED pattern (Fig. 4 j) indicates the polycrystalline nature with d-space values estimated as 0.12, 0.14, 0.20, 0.28 and 0.31 nm. The HRTEM imaging (SF 7 e) enables to view different lattice infringes with d-space values in agreement with that observed in SAED patterns. Since, the purified fractions are derived from ML as the common source, they have similarity of lattice points.

The characteristic ATR-IR spectra were acquired for PTCDA solid, NMP, ML (SF 8 a) and the separated fractions (SF 8 b). The precursor displays peaks at 730, 805 and 860 cm−1 due to C-H bending vibrations. The C-O and C=C stretching vibrations are observed at 1020, 1120 and 1300, 1407 cm−1 respectively. The two C=O of anhydride group appears at 1754 cm−1 in addition to = C-H stretch at 3123 cm−1. NMP exhibits peaks at 846 and 984 cm−1 corresponding to C-H bend, as well as 1109 and 1297 cm−1 for C-N and C-O stretching vibrations. Moreover, the peaks at 1407, 1662, 2880 and 2945 cm−1 are attributed to aliphatic C-H, C=O, C-H symmetric and asymmetric stretchings, respectively. The ML display peaks at 774, 850 and 984 cm−1 for C-H, 1109, 1300, 1407 and 1662 cm−1 corresponding to C-N, C-O, C=C and C=O stretching, respectively. The C-H symmetric and asymmetric stretches are observed at 2880 and 2945 cm−1. The column purified fractions show distinct peaks based on the characteristic functional groups of CDs and the eluting solvents (SF 8 b). The first and second fractions show peaks at 735 cm−1 due to C-Cl stretch as both exit the column upon addition of DCM. Besides, the peaks at 1260, 1430, 1684 and 3054 cm−1 are ascribed to C-O, C=C, C=O and C-H stretching vibrations. The latter fractions begin to separate after the addition of more polar solvent methanol. The third fraction exhibits peaks at 1115, 1411, 1650, 2840 and 2946 which corresponds to C-O, C=C, C=O, C-H symmetric and asymmetric stretches in addition to peaks at 1011 and 3308 cm−1 that belong to C-O bend and –OH stretch. The fourth and fifth fractions also show characteristic peaks similar to the third fraction indicating presence of similar functional groups. However, broad peak at 3295 cm−1 can indicate the possibility of H-bonding between –OH of CDs with the eluting solvent. The Supplementary Table (ST) 1 summarizes the peak values of PTCDA (solid), NMP, ML and all column purified fractions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface chemical state and elemental composition studies
ce:section.ce:para:
The elemental compositions of ML sample and the purified fractions acquired through XPS analysis are compared to deduce a correlation of surface groups with fluorescence features of column purified fractions. The survey scan of ML is overlaid with purified fractions as shown in the Fig. 5
a. The survey scan for ML has peaks centred at 285, 400, 532 and 1072 eV corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s and Na1s in addition to a peak at 900 eV belonging to C KLL subshell [21]. The elemental composition is estimated by component ratio analysis wherein the major element in ML is found to be O (42.71 %) followed by C (28.80 %), Na (24.05 %) and N (4.41 %). The elemental compositions of ML, second, third, fourth and fifth fractions are graphically represented in SF 9 and the values are summarised in ST 2.

The column purified fractions presented difference in composition of surface groups based on separation, which provides crucial information. The XPS analysis for first fraction was not performed as the yield of CDs was less. In the survey scan of second fraction, the peaks at 285 and 532 eV corresponds to C1s and O1s respectively, whereas the third fraction has designated peaks for C1s, N1s, O1s and Na1s at 285, 400, 532 and 1072 eV, indicating the incorporation of N content in the CDs of latter fractions. The elemental composition of third fraction indicates C (71.87 %) as the major surface group along with fewer compositions of O (13.62 %), N (1.78 %) and Na (1.03 %). The fourth fraction has spectral peaks corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s and Na1s at 285, 400, 532 and 1072 eV. Moreover, the same peaks are assigned for the fifth fraction as the elemental constituents are similar. The survey scans of fourth and fifth fractions have increased sodium content with increasing polarity. Deconvolution of the full scan spectra of ML, second, third, fourth and fifth fraction is performed and depicted in Fig. 5 b-l. In case of ML, second, third, fourth and fifth fractions the full scan spectrum of C1s was fitted with three Gaussian peaks corresponding to C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N and COOH. Similarly, deconvolution was performed on O1s to reveal C=O and C-O peaks. The full scan spectrum for N1s for fourth fraction was fitted with two peaks assigned at 399.7 eV for amino N and 401.5 eV for pyrrolic N. Graphical representation of elemental composition of C1s, O1s, N1s & Na1s for ML, second, third, fourth & fifth fractions are presented in SF 9.

The XRD peak for ML sample and the purified fractions are recorded and overlaid as shown in the Fig. 6
a. The ML exhibited sharp peaks indicating crystallinity (SF 10 a & ST 3). The peaks are centred at 30.5, 33.5, 45.9 and 53.2, and they overlap with graphite (gra_crb133_bo phase) carbon [22]. Moreover, the peaks at 45.9, 53.2, 56.6 and 75.1 match with reference compound (graphite corresponding to gra_crbl43_bo phase) (SF 10 b & ST 4). However, the unassigned peaks at 38.9, 44.0, 49.3, 54.9 and 60.3 arise due to the formation of intermediates in carbon allotropes. The plane of diffraction of the eluted fractions is different from the derived ML sample. The second fraction possesses broad peak indicating its amorphous nature. The third fraction show sharp peaks at 26.0, 31.7, 45.4, 66.2 and 75.4 which are compared with graphite (gra_crbl43_bo phase) reference peaks (SF 10 c & ST 5). The fourth fractions display peaks at 27.3, 31.8, 45.4, 56.3, 66.2 and 75.4 which match exactly with graphite (gra_crbl43_bo phase) reference peaks (SF 10 d & ST 6). This indicates that both third and fourth fractions are crystalline with similar planes of diffraction. However, the fifth fraction exhibits partly amorphous nature characterised by broad peak with few sharp peaks at 27.0 and 31.6 which corresponds to graphite (gra_crbl43_bo phase) reference compound (SF 10 e & ST 7).

Further analysis of the ML and separated fractions were accomplished by recording the Raman spectra. The Raman spectra of ML and purified (third, fourth and fifth) fractions were recorded at 785 nm excitation wavelength as shown in the Fig. 6 b. The spectra exhibit two distinct peaks namely, G-peak in the range of 1550 to 1600 cm−1 and D-peak in the range of 1300 to 1350 cm−1 [23]. The graphitic peak rises from in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon atoms while the distorted peak results from out-of-plane vibration of sp3 carbon atoms. The structural properties of carbon atoms can be deduced using the intensity ratios (ID/IG). The intensity ratios for all samples are closer or greater than unity. This indicates the presence of numerous functional groups on the carbon core with excess surface defects.

The extensive characterization of the five fractions provided insights on the polarity driven size-base separation during column chromatography. These findings are again proven based on the XPS data. The initial fractions eluted on addition of non-polar solvent have lesser composition of sodium whereas the latter fraction has major proportions of these polar ions. TEM imaging of all the fractions provides the existence of CDs that exhibit characteristic emissions. In addition, the first and third fractions display the presence of tubular CNRs suggesting separation based on size exclusion principle. Hence, purification becomes imperative, which could otherwise lead to erroneous interpretation of results when used as functional materials for various real-life applications [24–26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of fluorescent flexography ink using fifth fraction CDs and study of print properties for security printing
ce:section.ce:para: The different fractions that eluted out from the ML after hydrothermal treatment exhibited different fluorescence features, especially better QY in comparison to its derived ML sample. Due to the presence of numerous surface defects and chemical environments on the surface of CDs, they could exhibit multiple lifetime values [27,28]. Hence the fluorescence lifetime values of third, fourth and fifth fractions in liquid state were analysed to study the photostability of the CDs. The CDs belonging to third fraction possess emitters corresponding to λem = 400 and 570 nm having average lifetimes of 1.48 and 4.98 ns, respectively for λex = 360 nm. The emitters corresponding to λem = 570 nm display longer lifetime with mono-exponential decay nature (SF 11 a). Moreover, the emitters corresponding to λem = 400 nm possess bi-exponential decay and the population of these species exhibiting such lifetime is greater than λem = 570 nm emitters. All the emitters of fourth fraction exhibit single exponential decay as shown in SF 11 b. However, for λex = 390 nm, the emitters corresponding to λem = 560 and 575 nm display higher average lifetime values of 5.53 and 5.86 ns in comparison to emitters of λem = 486 nm. This suggests that longer wavelength emitters are slow to decay due to their longer lifetime values. The fifth fraction too exhibits mono-exponential decay plots as shown in SF 11 c & d as well as Fig. 6 c-e. However, the emitters in fifth fraction corresponding to λem = 490, 510 and 550 nm exhibit average lifetimes as high as 7.53, 7.86 and 7.58 ns for λex = 320 nm respectively. The ST 8 summarizes the individual and average lifetime values of CDs in third, fourth and fifth fractions for their inherent corresponding excitation and emission.

Solvent based inks are extensively used in flexography printing, and they pose environmental concerns due to the release of volatile organic compounds during ink manufacture or blending process. Therefore, considering the positive impact on environment, water-based inks formulations are developed with better adhesion to the print substrate [29,30]. The CDs were used in inkjet ink formulation and printed on paper to introduce covert features [31,32]. The inscribed QR code appeared invisible under daylight while displayed blue fluorescence when illuminated with a UV light source. Another research group printed dye-derived CDs on cotton cloth via inkjet printing technique. The patterns exhibited unique fluorescence under 365 and 395 nm light sources [33]. Multi-colour CDs were dispersed in Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and fluorescent flexible films were prepared which demonstrated desirable photostability. Moreover, the inkjet ink was printed on non-fluorescent paper which displayed blue, green, and red fluorescence under UV illumination [34]. Therefore, in the present study, the CDs of fraction five, which displays maximum fluorescence QY of 58.47 and showed better fluorescence stability in lifetime measurement studies were utilised to formulate an eco-friendly water-based fluorescent security ink for flexography printing. The fluorescence spectra (SF 12 a-b) of CDs in deionised water did not exhibit any major shifts in the emission compared to that in methanol. However, interestingly the QY of the CDs is found to drastically increase from 58.47 % to 88.60 % when they are transferred from methanol to water medium. This remarkable rise in QY prompted us to formulate the water-based flexo-ink by homogenously mixing the CDs in deionised water (pigment concentrate), surfactant, defoamer and wax. Then the CDs dispersion of pH 8 was dissolved in water soluble resin and sonicated to achieve the final homogenous ink formulation of pH 7.

The 3D fluorescence spectrum of flexo-ink displays the spectral region between 370–560 nm (SF 13 a). The corresponding 2D spectrum (Fig. 7
a) displays emissions at 490, 510 and 555 nm for excitations at 410, 440 and 470 nm. Moreover, the excitation in visible region (520 nm) yields emission at 590 nm. Further, the fluorescence spectrum for control ink formulation (devoid of CDs) was also recorded for similar excitations (Fig. 7 b). The control ink displays major emission at 435 nm suggesting that the presence of CDs creates profound changes in emission towards the visible region. The flexo ink and control formulation appear pale yellow and white, respectively under daylight and appear fluorescent green and blue, respectively under UV illumination as depicted in the inset Fig. 7 a-b.

The flow properties of printing inks significantly impact printability and print quality. Therefore, the rheological properties of flexo ink were evaluated by measuring viscosity with respect to varying shear rate. The viscosity of the ink was 4.50 cP at room temperature, which is ideal for the required flow and uptake by anilox roller. However, with increased shear rate (SF 13 b) 200 s−1 the ink exhibits deviations from Newtonian fluid behaviour (shear thickening). Based on this, the printability of flexo ink is better based on application of substantial amount of pressure during printing process. Under elevated temperatures, the ink demonstrates shear thinning (SF 13 c-e). Further increase in temperature results in drying of the ink.

Furthermore, the ink properties on UV dull paper were analysed initially by coating it using draw-down method and compared with that of the control ink film. The coated film of 4 mm thickness appears pale yellow under daylight and fluorescent green under 365 nm UV source. The CD coated paper displays emissions at 480, 520 and 560 nm for excitations corresponding to 410, 440 and 470 nm respectively (Fig. 7 c), similar to that of the ink formulation. To distinguish the emissions, the fluorescence spectrum of UV dull paper alone was obtained (SF 14 a), which shows maximum emissions at 430 and 540 nm corresponding to 370 nm excitation. Moreover, the control ink coated sample also exhibits similar fluorescence emissions as that of the blank paper (SF 14 b). Thus, emissions beyond 540 nm are from the CDs. Further, print proofs are obtained through flexography, which exhibits similar fluorescence peaks as observed in the coated sample indicating the uniform distribution of CDs on the paper surface (SF 13 f and Fig. 7 d). The photostability of the print proofs was evaluated by subjecting it to different (0, 10, 20, and 30) passes of UV exposure by placing 1.5 cm × 1.5 cm dimension print on UV curing unit at 300 W/30 m/min. The fluorescence spectra were recorded for these exposure cycles with excitations monitored at 410, 440 and 470 nm (SF 14 c-f). There is no significant decline in fluorescence intensity even after 10 to 30 passes. Hence, it can be confirmed that subsequent passes do not induce substantial quenching in fluorescence emissions.

Forged products/documents that are duplicated without prior consent or knowledge is a major global issue which leads to significant financial losses and has high societal impact. The good synthetic yield, high fluorescence QY and low photobleaching effect of the UV and blue light excitable hydrophilic CDs to display green and yellow fluorescent emission enables the prospective use of the environment friendly water-based ink in security printing. Many reports suggest the use of unpurified CDs as UV-excitable fluorescent pigments that display blue or green emissive prints from their aqueous solution, while few literature report emissions in the higher wavelengths or near-infrared region [35]. To explore the potential of the column purified CDs with high QY, good lifetime and attractive photobleaching features, ‘MIT’ was inscribed on UV dull paper and viewed under different excitation sources. The print appears pale yellow under daylight (Fig. 7 e), while it displays bright greenish yellow fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 7 f). The forger generally observes only the green fluorescence using the commonly used 365 nm excitation source. However, the user can also use a visible blue light to confirm the originality of the writing by observing a yellowish fluorescence when shined with blue light (Fig. 7 g). Thus, dual light excitable feature is an add on security element to improve the safety of products/documents. The benefit of using multiple excitations to display different emission colours from the printed region makes it difficult for forgers to replicate. Further to explore the use of this water-based ink in ink pads to place human fingerprints on important documents, the biocompatibility of the CDs was studied using MTT assay. The high percentage cell viability against the different studied concentration of CDs is portrayed in SF 15, which confirms the possible use of the formulation in ink pads.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of supercapacitor using CNRs of third fraction
ce:section.ce:para:
Carbon-based materials with high specific surface area and good chemical stability are widely used as electrode materials in electric double-layer capacitors. The third fraction that eluted out from the column constituted numerous CNRs in addition to CDs with diameter <10 nm and showed sharp peaks comparable to graphitic carbon. CNRs owing to their high surface area and length to-width ratio can be utilised in supercapacitor application [36]. Therefore, the third fraction carbonaceous nanomaterials were explored for their potential application in supercapacitors. The CV studies of pure SA, SA/CNRs and SA/CNRs/Mg was done using bare SS electrodes at different scan rates and are shown in Fig. 8
a-d. The CV patterns were found to be similar at different scan rates indicating structural stability of the polymer electrolytes. The comparison of CVs of pure SA, SA/CNRs and SA/CNRs/Mg showed an enhanced current window for SA/CNRs than the other two films which indicates a structural interaction of hydroxyl group of CNRs with the carboxylate group of SA. The addition of Mg salt induces the lowering of these interactions, thereby causing reduction in the current window. It is further evident in EIS studies (Fig. 8 e) that the conductivity of SA/CNRs/Mg is lesser than SA/CNRs due to the presence of Mg ions, while pure SA showed lesser conductivity. The ionic conductivity was calculated from the Rct intercept to real impedance axis. The dual ionic interaction of Na and Mg along with enhanced structural stability due to doped CNR makes it convenient for use in supercapacitor.

A supercapacitor was fabricated using SA/CNRs/Mg due to its higher conductivity. The CV of supercapacitor (Fig. 8 f) showed pseudocapacitive behaviour and displayed stability even after performing at various scan rates. A specific capacitance of 84F/g at 10 mV/s was obtained. The dual mechanism of double layer and redox behaviour may be due to the presence of easily reducible group in CNRs and SA, whereas Mg ions lead to double layer behaviour. The EIS spectrum of supercapacitor (Fig. 8 g) shows a semicircle pattern indicating capacitive nature and inclined straight line at high frequency region that demonstrates Warburg impedance [37]. GCD was performed at different current densities and Fig. 8 h shows initial 10 cycles of charge/discharge. The supercapacitor showed highest capacitance of 73F/g at 0.2 mA/g and displays inverted V shape with slight deviation indicating dual mechanism of double layer and redox behaviour. The GCD was performed for 2000 cycles and the coulombic efficiency was found to be 96 %. The high performance might be due to the CNRs acting as electronic boosters and maintaining the SA chains in a jelly form. Hence, Mg ions can be easily accessed on the interface of activated carbon and electrolyte. Further studies on importance of CNRs in enhancing the stability and electrochemical property is required and interaction with metal ions is of future interest.

The DSC thermograms of SA, SA/CNRs and SA/CNRs/Mg are shown in Fig. 9
a-c respectively. When observed for the peaks below 100 °C, SA, SA/CNRs and SA/CNRs/Mg showed peaks at 71, 74 and 72 °C, respectively. A shift of 3 °C upon addition of CNRs could be due to the interaction of SA with CNRs, thereby reducing its segmental motion. However, by inclusion of Mg salt, the ions were able to dominate the interactions of CNRs with SA, thereby reducing the temperature. Beyond, 190 °C all the three films started degrading and beyond 250 °C they started to decompose. It also indicates the complete dissociation of the doped Mg salt.

The SEM images of pure SA, SA/CNRs and SA/CNRs/Mg are shown in Fig. 9 d-f respectively. All the three electrolyte films were exposed to environment after film preparation. SA being bio-degradable in nature the growth of Aspergillus sp fungi on the surface of the film could be observed. Obviously, the polymer electrolyte when used for supercapacitor will be completely wrapped, thereby leading to decrease in the rate of degradation due to contamination. Interestingly, all the polymer electrolyte films including doped salts have shown characteristics of biodegradation, which forms the basis of an eco-friendly device. The pure SA film shows a uniform and relaxed surface as seen in the Fig. 9 d inset. With addition of CNRs the surface became heterogeneous due to aggregation of SA polar domains with functional groups of CNRs (Fig. 9 e). This suggests the existence of interaction between SA and CNRs. While in SA/CNRs/Mg (Fig. 9 c), the occurrence of phase separation as an indication of reduced pore size shows that the doped Mg salt was able to interact with the segments of SA and CNRs. This may explain the increase in ionic conductivity of SA/CNRs/Mg than SA/CNRs.

Multi-colour fluorescent CDs were synthesized from PTCDA via simple hydrothermal approach and five pure fluorescent fractions comprising of various carbonaceous nanostructures were separated via column chromatography using DCM-methanol solvent system. The five fractions separated exhibit unique fluorescence emissions and higher QY in comparison to the precursor. The TEM imaging revealed the presence of CNRs in the initial eluted fractions, while the latter fractions contained majorly CDs. Raman analysis proves the existence of both distorted and graphitic carbon while their ID/IG ratios indicate presence of several surface defects, attributed to numerous functional groups like COOH, OH and NH2 which is further confirmed by FT-IR and XPS data. The TCSPC results revealed mono-exponential decay with highest average lifetime of 7.86 ns for CDs in the fifth fraction. The eco-friendly water based ink formulated using fifth fraction with hydrophilic and biocompatible CDs with high absolute fluorescence quantum yield of 88.6 % could be used in security printing and ink pads. As the third fraction consists mainly of CNRs, an eco-friendly supercapacitor was fabricated using the CNRs. The dual ionic interaction of Na and Mg along with enhanced structural stability due to doped CNRs favored convenient device application. The fabricated supercapacitor displayed a specific capacitance of 84F/g with pseudocapacitance behaviour and was quite stable for 2000 cycles with 96 % coulombic efficiency.
